lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market studios whose primary business is repeat outsourced design work for external business or institutional customers across the NAICS 541490 specialties, including fashion, clothing, footwear, textile, jewelry, lighting, costume, and other residual professional design services. Included firms manage briefs, research, concepts, design development, technical specifications or production packages, revisions, and client delivery.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-house design teams, shells, non-control financing vehicles, firms outside the approximate $1-10M normalized EBITDA band, independent theatrical artists classified in NAICS 711510, design fields assigned to other NAICS codes, merchandise- or manufacturing-led businesses, and predominantly one-off bespoke consumer practices without repeat outsourced service relationships.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project, collection, season, campaign, or product-line design services sold mainly to brands, retailers, manufacturers, entertainment producers, developers, and institutions. Revenue may be fixed per project or deliverable, hourly, retainer-based, productized or subscription-based, and sometimes supplemented by change orders, licensing, royalties, or merchandise resale.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 541490 is a residual category spanning fashion, textile, footwear, jewelry, lighting, costume, float, and other design businesses whose customers, production processes, and economics differ materially. To preserve a coherent screen without selecting a favored specialty, the lens keeps repeat B2B outsourced design studios across all listed specialties and excludes goods-led, captive, and one-off consumer models.

a.bridge: The industry occupation source reports headcount, not wage-weighted task time: arts/design/media occupations are 47% of workers, office and administrative support 15%, management 7%, business and financial operations 6%, sales 7%, and production 7%. I mapped these groups to documented fashion-design and broader creative tasks, then applied broad relative-pay judgment to approximate wage weights. Exposed work includes trend and reference research, brainstorming, first-pass variants, sketches, mood boards, routine specifications, image finishing, proposals, administration, and sales support; less-exposed work includes client interpretation, taste and selection, material evaluation, physical prototypes, supplier interaction, and production oversight. This may overstate remaining opportunity because current creative professionals already use AI on many projects, and may understate it if emerging CAD and technical-package tools reach production reliability.
a.rationale: Design and support labor dominate the reported occupation mix, and much of the workflow is digital. Adobe's task research finds high AI enablement in brainstorming and ideation and substantial use in post-production, while a fashion implementation shows research, mood boards, concepts, and a tech pack can be accelerated dramatically. Yet creative labor is shifting toward selection, direction, client negotiation, provenance, and accountability, and fashion work still requires fabrics, prototypes, team collaboration, supplier knowledge, and final production oversight. The resulting wage-weighted exposure is material but below a majority in the central case after excluding work already automated.
a.change_evidence: Obtain anonymized time-and-payroll data from at least 30 LMM studios across fashion, textile, footwear, jewelry, lighting, and costume design. Map six months of hours by role and task, identify existing automation, and have practitioners test current AI tools on the remaining tasks with quality, revision, and approval outcomes.
caveats: The commercial occupation compilation says it uses US government data but displays graphic and interior designer shares even though those services are formally excluded from NAICS 541490, indicating classification or reporting noise.
Occupation shares are headcount rather than wages or task time, so wage weighting is judgmental.

The supplied compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized by a cross-industry median; specialty mix and merchandise contamination could make it unrepresentative of the narrowed service lens.
Creative-professional surveys include disciplines and firm sizes outside this industry.

rho.bridge: Adobe surveys measure use and perceived productivity among broader creative professionals rather than the five-year implemented share of exposed labor in LMM 541490 studios. More than 400 creative professionals reported using AI on over 40% of projects on average; 94% said they produced content faster, a majority said at least 50% faster, and average reported time saved was 17 hours weekly. A separate task study found AI concentrated in ideation and finishing while collaboration and physical work remained lightly used. I translate strong current deployment into a high-but-not-complete five-year implementation rate, discounting for self-selection, vendor sponsorship, intellectual-property risk, brand continuity, specialty-specific CAD, physical prototyping, and workflow change. The bridge likely overstates small-studio implementation if Adobe users are unusually AI-active, but could understate implementation as tools become embedded in standard design software.
rho.rationale: Implementation is already beyond experimentation for many digital creatives and is likely to spread through common software. Fashion-specific evidence demonstrates that trend research, mood boards, concepts, and technical packs can be integrated end to end. Constraints remain substantial: Adobe respondents reported concern about brand continuity and commercial safety, clients restrict AI in final commercial output, human contribution matters for copyright protection, and material or physical deliverables require review. Most exposed work should become assisted, but only part should become dependable labor substitution or avoided hiring.
rho.change_evidence: Track a panel of target studios for two years using software telemetry, project accounting, and staffing records. For each deployed AI workflow, measure hours avoided, additional iterations, rework, rights review, client rejection, freelancer displacement, and whether capacity gains reduced hiring or only increased output.
caveats: Adobe is a creative-software vendor, and its respondents are likely more digitally engaged than the full target population.
Reported time savings are self-assessed and do not establish net labor savings after prompt development, comparison, revision, provenance checks, and client review.
Fashion workflow evidence comes from a large retailer's proprietary system and may not transfer economically or operationally to LMM outsourced studios.
Implementation will vary widely across digital concept work, technical product design, lighting, jewelry, costume, and physical prototype activities.

e.rationale: The official definition establishes a professional-service category, which supports external outsourced work, but the catch-all spans materially different specialties. The commercial product list also shows merchandise resale, other receipts, and services formally associated with neighboring design codes, signaling mixed or noisy classification. The narrowed lens includes studios with repeat B2B engagements and operational teams; it excludes goods-led brands, captive design, bespoke consumer practices, and founder-only creative work. A central estimate modestly above half reflects the likely service orientation of employer firms while recognizing that recurring relationships and clean classification are not observed.
e.change_evidence: Create a named census of the 82 dataset-estimated LMM-band firms from Data Axle, D&B Hoovers, PitchBook, and state registrations. Review websites, invoices, and customer concentration to classify revenue by external design services, repeat clients, merchandise, licensing, and one-off consumer commissions, and test owner dependence through management interviews.
caveats: The supplied firm count is an ESTIMATE produced from size-class receipts and an EBITDA-margin bridge, not an observed list of firms.
No public source measures repeat-client or B2B revenue share for NAICS 541490 firms in the target EBITDA band.
The broad residual code and displayed neighboring-service revenue create material classification risk.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures plans among US business owners, not completed qualifying transfers among eligible specialized-design studios. In its 2024 survey, 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while owner-personal businesses were specifically cited as difficult to continue after retirement. I reduce the central estimate below 22% for noncompletion, gifts or reorganizations that may not transfer control, and the unusually high dependence of design firms on founder taste and client relationships. This may understate transfer likelihood for the LMM employer subset, which is larger and more operationally independent than the artists and nonemployers highlighted by Gallup.
s5.rationale: The target firms are employers at meaningful scale and therefore more transferable than solo creative practices, but the asset being sold often depends on a founder's creative direction, reputation, and relationships. Broad succession intentions create a credible pipeline, while failures to plan, client portability, key-person risk, and the absence of observed 541490 deal data limit confidence.
s5.change_evidence: Compile completed control transactions for NAICS 541490 and named fashion, textile, jewelry, lighting, and costume studios from PitchBook, GF Data, DealStats, Axial, BizBuySell, and trade press. Interview at least 30 eligible owners about age, exit horizon, ownership percentage, successor, client portability, and whether the firm can operate without the founder.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses and measures intentions rather than closed transactions.
The survey does not isolate professional design firms or the target EBITDA band.
Internal minority buy-ins, talent acquisitions without durable operations, and owner deaths without transfer are excluded from the primitive.

q.bridge: The pricing survey covers 273 US marketing, branding, design, creative, digital, and advertising agencies rather than only 541490 firms. Primary models were productized or subscription packages for 28.2%, hourly billing for 28.2%, project-by-project pricing for 25.3%, and fixed monthly retainers for 9.9%. I map fixed project, subscription, and retainer work to stronger initial retention and hourly work to immediate pass-through, then reduce five-year retention for renewal repricing, client demands for faster delivery, self-service of lower-stakes output, and competition from AI-enabled entrants. Agency data may overstate recurring packages for product and fashion studios, while founder differentiation and licensing economics could increase retention in some specialties.
q.rationale: Fixed deliverables, productized services, and retainers let studios retain near-term labor savings, while hourly contracts transmit fewer hours directly to customers. Over time, compressed deadlines, renegotiation, and clients' ability to generate acceptable low-stakes work themselves should share much of the benefit. Differentiated taste, trusted direction, commercial safety, and the ability to deliver more seasonal work preserve a meaningful operator share, but durable full capture is unlikely in an unlicensed and contestable service market.
q.change_evidence: Collect five years of contracts, invoices, proposal win rates, renewal prices, project hours, write-offs, licensing income, and change orders from at least 20 eligible studios. Compare realized price and margin for matched deliverables before and after AI deployment, segmented by fixed, hourly, retainer, subscription, royalty, and value-based terms.
caveats: The pricing evidence pools several agency types and reports the primary model per firm, not revenue share by contract type.

Client self-service belongs principally in operator-required demand, but it also increases competitive price pressure on retained projects; the estimate avoids counting eliminated volume as retained-benefit erosion.
Licensing, royalty, and merchandise economics are not quantified and vary sharply by specialty.

d5.bridge: BLS projects fashion-designer employment rather than constant-price, constant-quality demand for the narrowed NAICS 541490 service basket. Its 2% ten-year projection translates to about 1% over five years under a constant-growth assumption and anchors the base at 1.01. Census Service Annual Survey nominal taxable-employer revenue rose from $2.051 billion in 2019 to $2.890 billion in 2022, but that history is only directional context because it includes inflation and mix changes. Adobe's broader creative research reports rising hiring and content demand, which may overstate product-design demand, while fashion employment may understate other specialties such as lighting or entertainment design.
d5.rationale: The central case is essentially stable real service quantity. BLS cites a constant flow of new designs, social commerce, direct-to-consumer retail, and sustainable-material demand, but projects only modest fashion-designer employment growth. New collections, product refreshes, entertainment productions, and brand differentiation sustain work, while discretionary consumer categories, retail cycles, offshoring, in-house teams, and AI self-service create downside. The wide range reflects the residual code's disparate end markets.
d5.change_evidence: Build an inflation-adjusted five-year revenue and backlog panel for eligible firms by specialty and end market, using Census SAS/QSS, Bureau of Economic Analysis price indexes, fashion collection calendars, retail product-launch data, entertainment production starts, construction lighting-project starts, and direct firm bookings.
caveats: Fashion designers are only one occupation and many work in-house outside NAICS 541490.
The revenue series is nominal, taxable-employer only, ends in 2022, and does not isolate the narrowed repeat-B2B lens.
Creative content demand is adjacent to, but not the same as, fashion, textile, jewelry, footwear, lighting, costume, and other product-oriented design demand.
End-market cyclicality and specialty mix make one industry-wide quantity ratio unusually uncertain.

o.rationale: AI makes first-pass concepts and finishing easier, and Adobe's research reports clients already self-producing some lower-stakes work. Unlike licensed professional services, this category has little structural requirement that a specialized studio remain between software and the customer. Even so, repeat B2B work often requires brief interpretation, distinctive taste, material and manufacturing knowledge, prototype judgment, supplier coordination, revisions, commercial provenance, and accountability for final output. Copyright guidance also favors meaningful human creative control. The central estimate therefore assumes substantial self-service and software substitution at the low end but continued operator demand for differentiated, production-ready, and commercially defensible work.
o.change_evidence: Survey brand, retailer, manufacturer, entertainment, and institutional buyers about five-year insourcing and AI self-service plans by deliverable. Match buyer responses to actual RFP volumes, outsourced-design spend, vendor counts, and project cancellations, and audit which final assets require provenance, rights clearance, physical sampling, or supplier-ready specifications.
caveats: No source directly measures revenue-weighted software substitution for the narrowed NAICS 541490 lens.
Operator need differs sharply between a simple concept image and a manufacturable shoe, textile repeat, jewelry line, lighting plan, or costume package.
Copyrightability is only one form of commercial protection and does not by itself require an outside design firm.

narrative.executive_view: Other specialized design is a labor-heavy but unusually heterogeneous catch-all. Digital research, ideation, variation, finishing, documentation, and support work offer meaningful AI exposure, and creative professionals already report strong productivity. The counterweight is weak structural protection: customers can self-serve low-stakes concepts, AI-enabled entrants can compete faster, and founder taste or relationships can make firms difficult to transfer. Production-ready, materially informed, differentiated work remains more defensible than generic visual output.
narrative.ai_impact: AI is moving creative labor away from first-pass generation and post-production toward direction, selection, refinement, client negotiation, rights review, and accountability. In fashion-like workflows it can compress trend research, mood boards, concepting, and technical-package drafting; support functions can also automate proposals and administration. Physical sampling, material choice, fit, supplier coordination, lighting conditions, prototype review, and final production oversight remain substantially human and specialty-specific.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed projects, retainers, and productized packages can preserve early productivity gains, while hourly work passes savings through as fewer billed hours. Capture decays as clients demand compressed schedules, renew contracts, compare AI-enabled competitors, or generate acceptable low-stakes work internally. Studios retain more value where taste, speed, provenance, supplier-ready detail, and trusted accountability matter, but generic concepts are likely to reprice quickly.
narrative.firm_availability: The supplied dataset estimates 82 firms in the target EBITDA band, but the count is margin-bridged rather than a verified company list. Eligibility is reduced by the residual code's mixed specialties, neighboring-service misclassification, merchandise or manufacturing activity, one-off consumer work, and founder dependence. Broad employer-business succession evidence supports a plausible transfer pipeline, but there is no direct observed control-transfer series for this industry.
narrative.demand_durability: The base case is flat to modestly growing real demand, supported by continuing collection and product refresh cycles, social and direct-to-consumer commerce, sustainability requirements, entertainment production, and the need for differentiated commercial design. Downside comes from discretionary retail cycles, offshoring, in-house design, customer self-service, and commoditization of generic visual work. The outlook varies far more by specialty than the single NAICS label suggests.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest gaps are clean firm classification, true wage-weighted task time, production-grade hours avoided, revenue by pricing model, repeat-client incidence, and completed firm transfers. Vendor-sponsored creative surveys may overstate adoption and savings. Intellectual-property and brand-continuity concerns can slow implementation, while insufficient human authorship can weaken copyright protection. A roll-up could also acquire founder-dependent boutiques whose client relationships and creative identity do not survive control transfer.
narrative.principal_driver: A predominantly digital design-and-support workforce exposes research, ideation, variation, finishing, documentation, and administration to rapidly improving creative AI.
narrative.principal_weakness: The unlicensed, heterogeneous service basket faces customer self-service, rapid repricing, and founder-dependent transferability precisely where AI makes generic creative output abundant.

sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau NAICS: 541490 Other Specialized Design Services
sources.supports: Defines the residual professional-design category and lists costume, fashion, float, fur, jewelry, shoe, and textile design, while excluding architecture, landscape architecture, engineering, interior, industrial, graphic, computer-system, and independent theatrical design.

sources.title: Other Specialized Design Services - Market Size, Financial Statistics, Industry Trends
sources.supports: Displayed occupation mix includes arts/design/media 47%, office and administrative support 15%, management 7%, business and financial operations 6%, sales 7%, and production 7%; displayed product lines include clothing design, other design, merchandise resale, other receipts, and neighboring design services.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Fashion Designers
sources.supports: Documents fashion-design tasks including trend research, CAD, material selection, prototypes, presentations, marketing, and production oversight; reports 20% self-employment and projects 2% employment growth from 2024 to 2034, citing social commerce, direct-to-consumer sales, and sustainable materials.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Fashion Designers (27-1022.00)
sources.supports: Defines fashion designers and documents tasks such as sketches and specifications, examining and modifying sample garments, client and management discussions, material and production-technique selection, and arranging showings.

sources.title: Adobe AI and the Creative Frontier Study
sources.supports: Survey of 2,002 US creative professionals, developers, and freelancers: 90% believed generative AI could save time and money and create new ideas; 56% believed it could harm creators; large majorities sought attribution, labeling, and protections against unconsented training and infringement.

sources.title: Creatives Say AI Is Helping Them Meet Growing Demand for Content
sources.supports: Survey of more than 400 creative professionals and more than 400 marketers: creatives used AI on over 40% of projects on average; 94% said they produced faster, a majority said at least 50% faster, and average reported savings were 17 hours weekly; brand continuity and commercial safety remained major concerns.

sources.title: Adobe Research: How AI Is Redistributing Creative Work
sources.supports: Task research across creative professionals finds 81.7% of US creative pros rated brainstorming and ideation highly AI-enabled; AI concentrates at ideation and finishing while selection, direction, client communication, and accountability expand; clients self-produce some lower-stakes work, restrict final AI use, and still demand high-stakes output with provenance.

sources.title: Walmart: Trend-to-Product Uses GenAI in Fashion Design
sources.supports: Describes a fashion workflow in which AI condenses research and design from weeks to minutes, generates mood boards and a technical pack, and shortens concept-to-shelf timing by about 18 weeks while designers and merchants refine ideas and apply experience.

sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Fall 2024 survey found 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years; it also identifies personal-labor dependence, including artist businesses, as a barrier to continuation and distinguishes employer from nonemployer transferability.

sources.title: Agencies Split on Pricing Models
sources.supports: April 2025 survey of 273 US marketing, branding, design, creative, digital, and advertising agency leaders: 28.2% primarily used productized or subscription packages, 28.2% hourly, 25.3% project pricing, and 9.9% fixed monthly retainers; 45% adjusted prices at renewals.

sources.title: FRED Table Data: Total Revenue for Other Specialized Design Services, Taxable Employer Firms
sources.supports: US Census Service Annual Survey nominal revenue series for taxable employer firms, including $2.051 billion in 2019, $1.829 billion in 2020, $2.256 billion in 2021, and $2.890 billion in 2022; used only as historical directional context.

sources.title: U.S. Copyright Office Releases Part 2 of Artificial Intelligence Report
sources.supports: Copyright Office conclusion that generative-AI output is copyrightable only where a human determines sufficient expressive elements; human creative arrangement or modification may qualify, while prompts alone do not, and AI assistance does not bar copyrightability.
